keywords.description: SELECT - Retrieve data from tables. Foundation of SQL queries.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users; SELECT name, email FROM users WHERE id = 1;

keywords.description: FROM - Specifies table(s) to query data from.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users; SELECT u.name FROM users u;

keywords.description: WHERE - Filter rows based on conditions.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 18; WHERE active = true AND role = 'admin';

keywords.description: INSERT - Add new rows to a table.
keywords.example: INSERT INTO users (name, email) VALUES ('Alice', 'alice@example.com');

keywords.description: UPDATE - Modify existing rows in a table.
keywords.example: UPDATE users SET email = 'new@example.com' WHERE id = 1;

keywords.description: DELETE - Remove rows from a table.
keywords.example: DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1; DELETE FROM logs WHERE created_at < '2023-01-01';

keywords.description: CREATE - Create database objects (tables, indexes, views, etc.).
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100));

keywords.description: ALTER - Modify existing database objects.
keywords.example: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN email VARCHAR(255); ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN age;

keywords.description: DROP - Delete database objects. Irreversible operation.
keywords.example: DROP TABLE users; DROP INDEX idx_email; DROP DATABASE testdb;

keywords.description: TABLE - Keyword used with CREATE, ALTER, DROP to specify table operations.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (...); ALTER TABLE users ...; DROP TABLE users;

keywords.description: JOIN - Combine rows from two or more tables based on related columns.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

keywords.description: INNER JOIN - Returns matching rows from both tables.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users INNER JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

keywords.description: LEFT JOIN - Returns all rows from left table, matching rows from right (NULL if no match).
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users LEFT JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

keywords.description: RIGHT JOIN - Returns all rows from right table, matching rows from left (NULL if no match).
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM orders RIGHT JOIN users ON orders.user_id = users.id;

keywords.description: FULL JOIN - Returns all rows when there's a match in either table.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users FULL JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

keywords.description: CROSS JOIN - Cartesian product of two tables.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM colors CROSS JOIN sizes;

keywords.description: ON - Specifies join condition.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users JOIN orders ON users.id = orders.user_id;

keywords.description: USING - Shorthand for join when column names are identical.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users JOIN orders USING (user_id);

keywords.description: GROUP BY - Group rows with same values for aggregation.
keywords.example: SELECT country, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY country;

keywords.description: HAVING - Filter groups (WHERE for grouped data). Used after GROUP BY.
keywords.example: SELECT country, COUNT(*) FROM users GROUP BY country HAVING COUNT(*) > 10;

keywords.description: ORDER BY - Sort result set. ASC (default) or DESC.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY name ASC; ORDER BY age DESC, name ASC;

keywords.description: LIMIT - Restrict number of rows returned.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10; SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;

keywords.description: OFFSET - Skip specified number of rows before returning results.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users LIMIT 10 OFFSET 20;

keywords.description: DISTINCT - Return only unique values.
keywords.example: SELECT DISTINCT country FROM users; SELECT DISTINCT age, city FROM users;

keywords.description: AS - Create alias for columns or tables.
keywords.example: SELECT name AS user_name FROM users AS u; SELECT COUNT(*) AS total;

keywords.description: AND - Logical AND operator for combining conditions.
keywords.example: WHERE age > 18 AND country = 'USA';

keywords.description: OR - Logical OR operator for alternative conditions.
keywords.example: WHERE role = 'admin' OR role = 'moderator';

keywords.description: NOT - Logical negation operator.
keywords.example: WHERE NOT active = false; WHERE country NOT IN ('USA', 'Canada');

keywords.description: IN - Check if value matches any in a list.
keywords.example: WHERE country IN ('USA', 'Canada', 'UK'); WHERE id IN (SELECT user_id FROM orders);

keywords.description: BETWEEN - Check if value is within range (inclusive).
keywords.example: WHERE age BETWEEN 18 AND 65; WHERE created_at BETWEEN '2023-01-01' AND '2023-12-31';

keywords.description: LIKE - Pattern matching with wildcards. % matches any characters, _ matches single character.
keywords.example: WHERE name LIKE 'A%'; WHERE email LIKE '%@gmail.com'; WHERE code LIKE 'ABC_';

keywords.description: IS NULL - Check if value is NULL.
keywords.example: WHERE email IS NULL; WHERE deleted_at IS NULL;

keywords.description: IS NOT NULL - Check if value is not NULL.
keywords.example: WHERE email IS NOT NULL; WHERE last_login IS NOT NULL;

keywords.description: EXISTS - Check if subquery returns any rows.
keywords.example: WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders WHERE orders.user_id = users.id);

keywords.description: COUNT - Count number of rows. COUNT(*) includes NULLs, COUNT(column) excludes NULLs.
keywords.example: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users; SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT country) FROM users;

keywords.description: SUM - Calculate sum of numeric column.
keywords.example: SELECT SUM(amount) FROM orders; SELECT SUM(price * quantity) FROM order_items;

keywords.description: AVG - Calculate average of numeric column.
keywords.example: SELECT AVG(age) FROM users; SELECT AVG(rating) FROM reviews;

keywords.description: MIN - Find minimum value in column.
keywords.example: SELECT MIN(price) FROM products; SELECT MIN(created_at) FROM orders;

keywords.description: MAX - Find maximum value in column.
keywords.example: SELECT MAX(price) FROM products; SELECT MAX(updated_at) FROM users;

keywords.description: UPPER - Convert string to uppercase.
keywords.example: SELECT UPPER(name) FROM users; WHERE UPPER(email) = 'ADMIN@EXAMPLE.COM';

keywords.description: LOWER - Convert string to lowercase.
keywords.example: SELECT LOWER(name) FROM users; WHERE LOWER(country) = 'usa';

keywords.description: LENGTH - Get length of string.
keywords.example: SELECT LENGTH(name) FROM users; WHERE LENGTH(password) >= 8;

keywords.description: CONCAT - Concatenate strings.
keywords.example: SELECT CONCAT(first_name, ' ', last_name) AS full_name FROM users;

keywords.description: SUBSTRING - Extract substring from string.
keywords.example: SELECT SUBSTRING(email, 1, 5) FROM users; SUBSTRING(name FROM 1 FOR 10);

keywords.description: TRIM - Remove leading and trailing whitespace.
keywords.example: SELECT TRIM(name) FROM users; TRIM(BOTH ' ' FROM text);

keywords.description: COALESCE - Return first non-NULL value from list.
keywords.example: SELECT COALESCE(email, phone, 'N/A') FROM users;

keywords.description: CASE - Conditional expression (like if-then-else).
keywords.example: SELECT CASE WHEN age < 18 THEN 'minor' ELSE 'adult' END FROM users;

keywords.description: WHEN - Condition in CASE expression.
keywords.example: CASE WHEN age < 18 THEN 'minor' WHEN age < 65 THEN 'adult' ELSE 'senior' END

keywords.description: THEN - Result in CASE expression when condition is true.
keywords.example: CASE WHEN status = 'active' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END

keywords.description: ELSE - Default result in CASE expression.
keywords.example: CASE WHEN age < 18 THEN 'minor' ELSE 'adult' END

keywords.description: END - Closes CASE expression.
keywords.example: CASE WHEN condition THEN result ELSE default END

keywords.description: UNION - Combine results of multiple SELECT statements (removes duplicates).
keywords.example: SELECT name FROM users UNION SELECT name FROM admins;

keywords.description: UNION ALL - Combine results of multiple SELECT statements (keeps duplicates).
keywords.example: SELECT name FROM users UNION ALL SELECT name FROM guests;

keywords.description: INTERSECT - Return rows common to both SELECT statements.
keywords.example: SELECT email FROM users INTERSECT SELECT email FROM subscribers;

keywords.description: EXCEPT - Return rows from first SELECT not in second SELECT.
keywords.example: SELECT email FROM users EXCEPT SELECT email FROM unsubscribed;

keywords.description: PRIMARY KEY - Uniquely identifies each row. Cannot be NULL.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100));

keywords.description: FOREIGN KEY - Links to primary key in another table. Enforces referential integrity.
keywords.example: FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id);

keywords.description: UNIQUE - Ensures all values in column are different.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (email VARCHAR(255) UNIQUE);

keywords.description: NOT NULL - Column cannot contain NULL values.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (name VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL);

keywords.description: CHECK - Validates data before insert/update.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (age INT CHECK (age >= 18));

keywords.description: DEFAULT - Provides default value for column.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true);

keywords.description: AUTO_INCREMENT - Automatically generates unique number for new rows (MySQL).
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (id INT AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY);

keywords.description: INDEX - Create index for faster queries.
keywords.example: CREATE INDEX idx_email ON users(email); CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_username ON users(username);

keywords.description: VIEW - Virtual table based on SELECT query.
keywords.example: CREATE VIEW active_users AS SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = true;

keywords.description: TRANSACTION - Group of SQL operations executed as single unit.
keywords.example: BEGIN TRANSACTION; ... COMMIT; / ROLLBACK;

keywords.description: COMMIT - Save all changes made in current transaction.
keywords.example: BEGIN TRANSACTION; UPDATE users SET active = true; COMMIT;

keywords.description: ROLLBACK - Undo all changes made in current transaction.
keywords.example: BEGIN TRANSACTION; DELETE FROM users; ROLLBACK;

keywords.description: INT - Integer number type.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (age INT);

keywords.description: VARCHAR - Variable-length string with maximum length.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (name VARCHAR(255));

keywords.description: TEXT - Large text data type.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE posts (content TEXT);

keywords.description: DATE - Date value (YYYY-MM-DD).
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE events (event_date DATE);

keywords.description: DATETIME - Date and time value.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE logs (created_at DATETIME);

keywords.description: TIMESTAMP - Timestamp value, often with timezone.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP);

keywords.description: BOOLEAN - True/false value.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true);

keywords.description: DECIMAL - Fixed-point number with precision and scale.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE products (price DECIMAL(10, 2));

keywords.description: BIGINT - Large integer type. Range: -9223372036854775808 to 9223372036854775807.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE events (id BIGINT PRIMARY KEY);

keywords.description: SMALLINT - Small integer type. Range: -32768 to 32767.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE settings (priority SMALLINT DEFAULT 0);

keywords.description: FLOAT - Approximate floating-point number.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE measurements (value FLOAT);

keywords.description: REAL - Single-precision floating-point number.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE sensors (reading REAL);

keywords.description: NUMERIC - Exact numeric type with user-specified precision. Alias for DECIMAL.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE invoices (amount NUMERIC(12, 2));

keywords.description: CHAR - Fixed-length character string, padded with spaces.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE countries (code CHAR(2) NOT NULL);

keywords.description: BLOB - Binary Large Object for storing binary data.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE files (data BLOB);

keywords.description: JSON - Store and query JSON data natively.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE configs (settings JSON); SELECT settings->>'name' FROM configs;

keywords.description: JSONB - Binary JSON format (PostgreSQL). Faster queries, supports indexing.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE docs (data JSONB); SELECT data @> '{"key": "value"}' FROM docs;

keywords.description: UUID - Universally Unique Identifier type.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (id UUID DEFAULT gen_random_uuid() PRIMARY KEY);

keywords.description: SERIAL - Auto-incrementing integer (PostgreSQL). Equivalent to INT + sequence.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY);

keywords.description: INTERVAL - Represents a time span or duration.
keywords.example: SELECT NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'; WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '1 hour';

keywords.description: XML - Store and query XML data.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE docs (content XML);

keywords.description: ARRAY - Store arrays of values (PostgreSQL). Supports indexing and functions.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE users (tags TEXT[]); SELECT * FROM users WHERE 'admin' = ANY(tags);

keywords.description: BYTEA - Binary data type (PostgreSQL). For raw byte strings.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE files (content BYTEA);

keywords.description: MONEY - Currency type with locale-aware formatting.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE products (price MONEY);

keywords.description: ENUM - Enumerated type with a fixed set of allowed values.
keywords.example: CREATE TYPE status AS ENUM ('active', 'inactive', 'pending');

keywords.description: ROW_NUMBER - Assigns sequential integer to rows within a partition.
keywords.example: SELECT ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY created_at) AS rn FROM users;

keywords.description: RANK - Assigns rank with gaps for ties within a partition.
keywords.example: SELECT RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS rank FROM students;

keywords.description: DENSE_RANK - Assigns rank without gaps for ties within a partition.
keywords.example: SELECT DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY score DESC) AS rank FROM students;

keywords.description: NTILE - Distributes rows into a specified number of roughly equal groups.
keywords.example: SELECT NTILE(4) OVER (ORDER BY salary) AS quartile FROM employees;

keywords.description: LAG - Access value from a previous row without self-join.
keywords.example: SELECT amount, LAG(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS prev_amount FROM sales;

keywords.description: LEAD - Access value from a subsequent row without self-join.
keywords.example: SELECT amount, LEAD(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS next_amount FROM sales;

keywords.description: FIRST_VALUE - Returns the first value in a window frame.
keywords.example: SELECT FIRST_VALUE(name) OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) FROM employees;

keywords.description: LAST_VALUE - Returns the last value in a window frame.
keywords.example: SELECT LAST_VALUE(name) OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND UNBOUNDED FOLLOWING) FROM employees;

keywords.description: NTH_VALUE - Returns the value at the nth row of the window frame.
keywords.example: SELECT NTH_VALUE(salary, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary DESC) FROM employees;

keywords.description: OVER - Defines a window for window functions. Specifies partitioning and ordering.
keywords.example: SELECT SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY date) FROM orders;

keywords.description: PARTITION BY - Divides rows into groups for window functions.
keywords.example: SELECT ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY department ORDER BY salary DESC) FROM employees;

keywords.description: ROWS BETWEEN - Defines window frame boundaries by physical row position.
keywords.example: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)

keywords.description: RANGE BETWEEN - Defines window frame boundaries by logical value range.
keywords.example: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date RANGE BETWEEN INTERVAL '7' DAY PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)

keywords.description: UNBOUNDED PRECEDING - Window frame boundary: from the first row of the partition.
keywords.example: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)

keywords.description: CURRENT ROW - Window frame boundary: the current row.
keywords.example: SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date ROWS BETWEEN 3 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW)

keywords.description: NOW - Returns current date and time.
keywords.example: SELECT NOW(); WHERE created_at > NOW() - INTERVAL '24 hours';

keywords.description: CURRENT_TIMESTAMP - Returns current date and time (SQL standard).
keywords.example: SELECT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP; DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP;

keywords.description: CURRENT_DATE - Returns current date without time.
keywords.example: SELECT CURRENT_DATE; WHERE event_date = CURRENT_DATE;

keywords.description: CURRENT_TIME - Returns current time without date.
keywords.example: SELECT CURRENT_TIME;

keywords.description: EXTRACT - Extract part (year, month, day, hour, etc.) from date/timestamp.
keywords.example: SELECT EXTRACT(YEAR FROM created_at) FROM orders; EXTRACT(MONTH FROM date);

keywords.description: DATE_TRUNC - Truncate timestamp to specified precision (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: SELECT DATE_TRUNC('month', created_at) FROM orders; GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('day', ts);

keywords.description: DATE_ADD - Add interval to date/timestamp (MySQL).
keywords.example: SELECT DATE_ADD(created_at, INTERVAL 30 DAY) FROM users;

keywords.description: DATEDIFF - Calculate difference between two dates.
keywords.example: SELECT DATEDIFF(end_date, start_date) AS days_diff FROM projects;

keywords.description: DATE_FORMAT - Format date/timestamp as string (MySQL).
keywords.example: SELECT DATE_FORMAT(created_at, '%Y-%m-%d') FROM orders;

keywords.description: TO_CHAR - Convert date/number to formatted string (PostgreSQL/Oracle).
keywords.example: SELECT TO_CHAR(created_at, 'YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS') FROM orders;

keywords.description: TO_DATE - Parse string into date (PostgreSQL/Oracle).
keywords.example: SELECT TO_DATE('2024-01-15', 'YYYY-MM-DD');

keywords.description: ROUND - Round number to specified decimal places.
keywords.example: SELECT ROUND(price, 2) FROM products; SELECT ROUND(AVG(rating), 1) FROM reviews;

keywords.description: CEIL / CEILING - Round up to nearest integer.
keywords.example: SELECT CEIL(4.2); -- returns 5

keywords.description: FLOOR - Round down to nearest integer.
keywords.example: SELECT FLOOR(4.8); -- returns 4

keywords.description: ABS - Return absolute value of a number.
keywords.example: SELECT ABS(-5); -- returns 5; SELECT ABS(balance) FROM accounts;

keywords.description: MOD - Return remainder of division.
keywords.example: SELECT MOD(10, 3); -- returns 1; WHERE MOD(id, 2) = 0;

keywords.description: POWER - Raise number to a power.
keywords.example: SELECT POWER(2, 10); -- returns 1024

keywords.description: SQRT - Return square root of a number.
keywords.example: SELECT SQRT(144); -- returns 12

keywords.description: REPLACE - Replace occurrences of a substring.
keywords.example: SELECT REPLACE(email, '@old.com', '@new.com') FROM users;

keywords.description: LEFT - Extract leftmost characters from string.
keywords.example: SELECT LEFT(name, 3) FROM users; -- first 3 characters

keywords.description: RIGHT - Extract rightmost characters from string.
keywords.example: SELECT RIGHT(phone, 4) FROM users; -- last 4 digits

keywords.description: LPAD - Pad string on the left to a specified length.
keywords.example: SELECT LPAD(id::text, 5, '0') FROM orders; -- '00042'

keywords.description: RPAD - Pad string on the right to a specified length.
keywords.example: SELECT RPAD(name, 20, '.') FROM users;

keywords.description: POSITION - Find position of substring within string (1-indexed).
keywords.example: SELECT POSITION('@' IN email) FROM users;

keywords.description: REVERSE - Reverse characters in a string.
keywords.example: SELECT REVERSE('hello'); -- 'olleh'

keywords.description: SPLIT_PART - Split string by delimiter and return nth part (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: SELECT SPLIT_PART(email, '@', 2) AS domain FROM users;

keywords.description: CAST - Convert value from one data type to another.
keywords.example: SELECT CAST(price AS INT) FROM products; CAST('2024-01-01' AS DATE);

keywords.description: NULLIF - Returns NULL if two expressions are equal, otherwise the first.
keywords.example: SELECT NULLIF(discount, 0) FROM products; -- avoid division by zero

keywords.description: GREATEST - Return the largest value from a list of expressions.
keywords.example: SELECT GREATEST(score1, score2, score3) AS best_score FROM results;

keywords.description: LEAST - Return the smallest value from a list of expressions.
keywords.example: SELECT LEAST(price, max_budget) AS effective_price FROM products;

keywords.description: STRING_AGG - Concatenate strings with delimiter (PostgreSQL). Like GROUP_CONCAT.
keywords.example: SELECT STRING_AGG(name, ', ' ORDER BY name) FROM users GROUP BY department;

keywords.description: GROUP_CONCAT - Concatenate strings within a group (MySQL).
keywords.example: SELECT GROUP_CONCAT(name SEPARATOR ', ') FROM users GROUP BY department;

keywords.description: ARRAY_AGG - Aggregate values into an array (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: SELECT department, ARRAY_AGG(name) FROM employees GROUP BY department;

keywords.description: JSON_AGG - Aggregate values into a JSON array (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: SELECT department, JSON_AGG(name) FROM employees GROUP BY department;

keywords.description: FILTER - Apply condition to aggregate function (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: SELECT COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE active = true) AS active_count FROM users;

keywords.description: WITH - Define Common Table Expressions (CTEs) for readable subqueries.
keywords.example: WITH active AS (SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = true) SELECT * FROM active;

keywords.description: RECURSIVE - Create recursive CTE for hierarchical or iterative queries.
keywords.example: WITH RECURSIVE tree AS (SELECT * FROM categories WHERE parent_id IS NULL UNION ALL SELECT c.* FROM categories c JOIN tree ON c.parent_id = tree.id) SELECT * FROM tree;

keywords.description: LATERAL - Subquery that can reference columns from preceding FROM items.
keywords.example: SELECT u.*, l.* FROM users u, LATERAL (SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = u.id LIMIT 3) l;

keywords.description: ASC - Sort in ascending order (default for ORDER BY).
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY name ASC;

keywords.description: DESC - Sort in descending order.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY created_at DESC;

keywords.description: FETCH - SQL standard alternative to LIMIT. Used with OFFSET.
keywords.example: SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY id OFFSET 10 ROWS FETCH NEXT 5 ROWS ONLY;

keywords.description: ALL - Compare a value to every value in a subquery result.
keywords.example: WHERE price > ALL (SELECT price FROM products WHERE category = 'basic');

keywords.description: ANY / SOME - Compare a value to any value in a subquery result.
keywords.example: WHERE price > ANY (SELECT price FROM products WHERE category = 'basic');

keywords.description: ILIKE - Case-insensitive pattern matching (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: WHERE name ILIKE '%john%'; WHERE email ILIKE '%@gmail.com';

keywords.description: SIMILAR TO - Pattern matching using SQL regular expressions.
keywords.example: WHERE name SIMILAR TO '(John|Jane)%';

keywords.description: TRUNCATE - Remove all rows from a table quickly. Faster than DELETE, cannot be rolled back in some databases.
keywords.example: TRUNCATE TABLE logs; TRUNCATE TABLE sessions CASCADE;

keywords.description: MERGE - Perform INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE in a single statement based on conditions.
keywords.example: MERGE INTO target USING source ON target.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET ... WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT ...;

keywords.description: RETURNING - Return data from modified rows (PostgreSQL). Available with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE.
keywords.example: INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Alice') RETURNING id; DELETE FROM users WHERE id = 1 RETURNING *;

keywords.description: ON CONFLICT - Handle insert conflicts / upsert (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: INSERT INTO users (email, name) VALUES ('a@b.com', 'Alice') ON CONFLICT (email) DO UPDATE SET name = EXCLUDED.name;

keywords.description: EXPLAIN - Show query execution plan for performance analysis.
keywords.example: EXPLAIN SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'test@example.com'; EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT ...;

keywords.description: ANALYZE - Collect statistics for query optimizer / execute and show actual plan.
keywords.example: ANALYZE users; EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM users;

keywords.description: VACUUM - Reclaim storage and update planner statistics (PostgreSQL).
keywords.example: VACUUM; VACUUM FULL users; VACUUM ANALYZE;

keywords.description: CASCADE - Automatically apply operation to dependent objects.
keywords.example: DROP TABLE users CASCADE; ALTER TABLE users DROP COLUMN id CASCADE;

keywords.description: RESTRICT - Prevent operation if dependent objects exist.
keywords.example: DROP TABLE users RESTRICT;

keywords.description: TEMPORARY / TEMP - Create temporary table that exists only for the session.
keywords.example: CREATE TEMPORARY TABLE temp_results AS SELECT * FROM results WHERE score > 90;

keywords.description: IF EXISTS - Only execute DDL if object exists. Prevents errors.
keywords.example: DROP TABLE IF EXISTS old_users; DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_email;

keywords.description: IF NOT EXISTS - Only create object if it doesn't already exist.
keywords.example: CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS users (id INT PRIMARY KEY); CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_email ON users(email);

keywords.description: SCHEMA - Namespace for database objects. Groups tables, views, etc.
keywords.example: CREATE SCHEMA analytics; SELECT * FROM analytics.events;

keywords.description: DATABASE - Container for schemas, tables, and other objects.
keywords.example: CREATE DATABASE myapp; DROP DATABASE IF EXISTS testdb;

keywords.description: SEQUENCE - Auto-incrementing number generator.
keywords.example: CREATE SEQUENCE user_id_seq START 1 INCREMENT 1; SELECT nextval('user_id_seq');

keywords.description: MATERIALIZED VIEW - View that stores query results physically. Must be refreshed.
keywords.example: CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales AS SELECT ...; REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW monthly_sales;

keywords.description: GRANT - Give privileges on database objects to users or roles.
keywords.example: GRANT SELECT, INSERT ON users TO app_user; GRANT ALL ON DATABASE mydb TO admin;

keywords.description: REVOKE - Remove privileges from users or roles.
keywords.example: REVOKE INSERT ON users FROM app_user; REVOKE ALL ON DATABASE mydb FROM guest;

keywords.description: SAVEPOINT - Create a point within a transaction to partially roll back to.
keywords.example: SAVEPOINT sp1; -- ... operations ... ROLLBACK TO sp1;

keywords.description: BEGIN - Start a transaction block.
keywords.example: BEGIN; UPDATE accounts SET balance = balance - 100 WHERE id = 1; COMMIT;

keywords.description: PROCEDURE - Stored procedure for reusable SQL logic.
keywords.example: CREATE PROCEDURE transfer(sender INT, receiver INT, amount DECIMAL) ...

keywords.description: FUNCTION - User-defined function that returns a value.
keywords.example: CREATE FUNCTION full_name(first TEXT, last TEXT) RETURNS TEXT AS $$ ... $$;

keywords.description: TRIGGER - Automatically execute code in response to table events.
keywords.example: CREATE TRIGGER update_timestamp BEFORE UPDATE ON users FOR EACH ROW EXECUTE FUNCTION update_modified();

keywords.description: DECLARE - Declare variables or cursors in procedural blocks.
keywords.example: DECLARE @count INT; DECLARE cur CURSOR FOR SELECT id FROM users;

keywords.description: CURSOR - Database object for traversing result sets row by row.
keywords.example: DECLARE cur CURSOR FOR SELECT id, name FROM users; OPEN cur; FETCH NEXT FROM cur;

keywords.description: EXECUTE - Execute a prepared statement or dynamic SQL.
keywords.example: PREPARE stmt AS SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = $1; EXECUTE stmt(42);

keywords.description: RETURNS - Specify return type of a function.
keywords.example: CREATE FUNCTION get_count() RETURNS INTEGER AS $$ SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users; $$;

snippets.content: SELECT * FROM ${1:table_name};

SELECT ${1:column1}, ${2:column2}
FROM ${3:table_name};

snippets.description: Select with GROUP BY and HAVING

snippets.content:
INSERT INTO ${1:table_name} (${2:column1}, ${3:column2})
VALUES (${4:value1}, ${5:value2});

INSERT INTO ${1:table_name} (${2:column1}, ${3:column2})
VALUES 
	(${4:value1}, ${5:value2}),
	(${6:value3}, ${7:value4});

DELETE FROM ${1:table_name}
WHERE ${2:condition};

ALTER TABLE ${1:table_name}
ADD COLUMN ${2:column_name} ${3:datatype} ${4:constraints};

ALTER TABLE ${1:table_name}
DROP COLUMN ${2:column_name};

ALTER TABLE ${1:table_name}
MODIFY COLUMN ${2:column_name} ${3:new_datatype} ${4:constraints};

CREATE INDEX ${1:idx_name}
ON ${2:table_name}(${3:column_name});

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX ${1:idx_name}
ON ${2:table_name}(${3:column_name});

SELECT COUNT(*) as ${1:total}
FROM ${2:table_name};

SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT ${1:column}) as ${2:unique_count}
FROM ${3:table_name};

SELECT 
	COUNT(*) as ${1:count},
	SUM(${2:column}) as ${3:total},
	AVG(${2:column}) as ${4:average},
	MIN(${2:column}) as ${5:minimum},
	MAX(${2:column}) as ${6:maximum}
FROM ${7:table_name};

snippets.description: Compare with previous/next row using LAG and LEAD
Complete SELECT with WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, ORDER BY, LIMIT

snippets.content:
INSERT INTO ${1:table_name} (${2:column1}, ${3:column2})
VALUES (${4:value1}, ${5:value2})
ON CONFLICT (${6:conflict_column})
DO UPDATE SET
	${3:column2} = EXCLUDED.${3:column2};

snippets.description: INSERT ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE (MySQL upsert)
snippets.content:
INSERT INTO ${1:table_name} (${2:column1}, ${3:column2})
VALUES (${4:value1}, ${5:value2})
ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE
	${3:column2} = VALUES(${3:column2});

CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ${1:table_name} (
	${2:id} SERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
	${3:column1} ${4:VARCHAR(255)} NOT NULL,
	${5:column2} ${6:TEXT},
	${7:created_at} TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW(),
	${8:updated_at} TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW()
);

ALTER TABLE ${1:table_name}
RENAME COLUMN ${2:old_name} TO ${3:new_name};

ALTER TABLE ${1:old_table_name}
RENAME TO ${2:new_table_name};

CREATE INDEX ${1:idx_name}
ON ${2:table_name}(${3:column1}, ${4:column2});

GRANT ${1:SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE} ON ${2:table_name}
TO ${3:role_name};

REVOKE ${1:INSERT, UPDATE} ON ${2:table_name}
FROM ${3:role_name};

EXPLAIN ANALYZE
${1:SELECT * FROM table_name WHERE condition};
TRUNCATE TABLE ${1:table_name} ${2:CASCADE};

snippets.description: Pagination with OFFSET and FETCH (SQL standard)
Select first row per group (PostgreSQL DISTINCT ON)

snippets.content: CAST(${1:expression} AS ${2:datatype})
COALESCE(${1:column}, ${2:default_value})
